content:
I wanted to float https://android-review.git.corp.google.com/c/kernel/common/+/2811635 with y'all before I send it to the list, since the list will have it going to the kconfig maintainers, and I don't know whether the Rust maintainers would be supportive of it.

I'm going to reword the commit message, but the basic gist of it is that it pulls Rust symbols off the kernel surface in exchange for disabling the ability to build Rust kernel modules. This lets us turn MODVERSIONS back on if we're only using Rust in a built-in capacity.

Full disclosure - the primary reason I'm interested in this is that the Android kernel demands that the KMI (kernel module interface) be ABI stable, which means that the current design of Rust modules can't ship enabled in Android. In the (hopefully near) future I'd like to make modules workable, but that's a much more complex change, and I'd like to get a minimal set of changes that lets Alice's binder work land.
Sounds good to me :+1:
It would be great to have versioned Rust symbols as well though

I'm not sure that we can have versioned rust symbols.
What I'm exploring for MODVERSIONS + RUST_MODULES is the possibility of lifting the subsystem bindings out of the main kernel image and into the built modules themselves, or into a support module, but that's not in a reviewable state yet.
Maybe I need to educate myself a bit more. I thought MODVERSIONS was a versioning of the symbols. To make sure that modules being loaded are build for the running kernel?
Sounds like I’m wrong in my assumption though
@Matthew Maurer Maybe you should collaborate with Canonical to get modversion actually enabled for Rust symbols?
MODVERSIONS is a versioning of symbols, but due to .rmeta, I don't think you can usefully depend on symbol versions for compatibility

I'll stop by the meeting today, if you're there maybe we can chat so I can understand what you're trying to do. Technical support for "we can emit a version for rust symbols" will not necessarily produce the functionality MODVERSIONS is intended to provide.

Between different rustc versions we emit unique symbol names. Within a rustc version we are only guaranteed to emit unique symbol names if the -Cmetadata argument passed when compiling a crate differs. Otherwise the symbol name could be identical while still being ABI incompatible. In any case the rmeta file contains MIR for all generic and #[inline] functions to be codegened in the crate that uses it as well as on recent nighlies MIR for any function which rustc deems to be useful to inline cross-crate.

https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/2023112312-certified-substance-007c@gregkh/T/#t has been posted for MODVERSIONS support with rust. Since my last reply in this topic, a major change in rustc has happened: Even without #[inline], rustc can now inline functions cross-crate if it thinks they are small enough to be worthwhile to inline. I think this effectively means that the function signature CRCs for rust functions will have to be derived from a hash of the entire crate rather than the signature of the individual function as basically any function may have been inlined and thus can't safely be swapped out between kernel versions. It should still be fine if the respective crate and it's dependencies hasn't been modified though, so hashing just the .rmeta file of the crate that defined the function should be fine I think. The .rmeta file contains the crate hashes for all dependencies already.

Hey Guys! My question is "Is there a way to create a rust module as an ko file (loadable module)". I'm experimenting with some code and I wanted to try that approach. I think Wedson mentionned in his mentorship video that this approach is possible. Is there any steps that I should follow to get That.
Note: I didn't know in which topic this question lays so I put it here as it seemed the right place
I think there are examples around. If not, you can look at null block

Matthew Maurer said:

Full disclosure - the primary reason I'm interested in this is that the Android kernel demands that the KMI (kernel module interface) be ABI stable, which means that the current design of Rust modules can't ship enabled in Android. In the (hopefully near) future I'd like to make modules workable, but that's a much more complex change, and I'd like to get a minimal set of changes that lets Alice's binder work land.

I’m curious if requiring Android vendors to ship their proprietary drivers as userspace drivers (using VFIO and an SMMU) has been considered.

Ideal would obviously be to require all modules to be upstreamed prior to product release, as Intel does, but that isn’t going to happen in the near future (except, hopefully, for Pixels).
